1. Jamie Dimon (JPMorgan Chase, CEO)  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know & trust level:  
   – Veteran banking executive, head of the largest U.S. bank by assets.  
   – Publicly very critical of Bitcoin (“fraud,” “speculation”), reflecting incumbent-bank bias.  
   – I trust his macro-financial judgment on interest rates, credit cycles, traditional markets—moderately high—but I discount his crypto bearishness because he’s defending legacy banking profits.  

3. Cathie Wood (ARK Invest, CEO & CIO)  
1. Do I know her? Yes.  
2. What I know & trust level:  
   – Well-known for aggressive, research-driven bets on disruptive tech (Tesla, genomics, blockchain).  
   – Her funds have occasional big wins but overall underperformed benchmarks in recent years.  
   – I respect the depth of ARK’s thematic research but view her $500 K Bitcoin call as very optimistic—trust in her conviction but lean more skeptical on magnitude and timeline.  

4. Larry Fink (BlackRock, CEO)  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know & trust level:  
   – Head of the world’s largest asset manager; very measured, data-driven, long-term focus.  
   – Has moved toward Bitcoin by filing for a spot-BTC ETF—signals institutional adoption.  
   – I give his views moderately high credibility: he’s unlikely to hype, and if he’s predicting $150 K, he’s factoring in serious inflows and utility.  

5. Peter Schiff (Euro Pacific Capital, Host)  
1. Do I know him? Yes.  
2. What I know & trust level:  
   – Prominent gold-bug and vocal Bitcoin skeptic.  
   – Publishes daily podcasts, repeats anti-crypto arguments; has been wrong on major Bitcoin rallies.  
   – I assign his judgment low credibility on Bitcoin—he’s too narrowly focused on gold and tends to dismiss any crypto upside.  

Bottom line:  
– Dimon and Schiff carry strong incumbent or niche-bias views—worth knowing but I discount their extremes.  
– Wood’s deeply researched but aggressively bullish stance I respect, though I temper her upside.  
– Fink’s middle-of-the-road, institution-driven view feels most balanced.

2. Cathie Wood ($500,000)  
   1. Do I know her? Yes.  
   2. What I know and trust level:  
      • Founder of ARK Invest, famous for high-conviction bets on disruptive tech (Tesla, genomics, etc.).  
      • Her funds can be extremely volatile, but she’s transparent about her models and conviction.  
      • She has a track record of big up-moves in bull markets and steep drawdowns when momentum shifts.  
      Trust on Bitcoin: moderate. I like that she shares her assumptions publicly, but I’m cautious about extrapolating institutional adoption curves so aggressively.  

3. Larry Fink ($150,000)  
   1. Do I know him? Yes.  
   2. What I know and trust level:  
      • CEO of BlackRock, the world’s largest asset manager. He’s been more open to crypto in recent years, even offering Bitcoin ETFs.  
      • His edge is deep client insight and asset-allocation data—he sees real flows.  
      • He’s neither a maximalist nor a hard skeptic; his clients drove him toward crypto exposure.  
      Trust on Bitcoin: moderate-high. He’s less ideologically driven than Dimon or Schiff, and BlackRock’s institutional lens gives his forecast extra weight for me.  

4. Peter Schiff ($3,000)  
   1. Do I know him? Yes.  
   2. What I know and trust level:  
      • A longtime gold bull and Bitcoin skeptic, runs Euro Pacific Capital and a popular podcast.  
      • He’s consistent in warning of bubbles and financial crises—but his track record on crisis-timing is mixed.  
      • He has a clear conflict: promoting gold to the detriment of crypto.  
      Trust on Bitcoin: low. His arguments are articulate but filtered through a gold-centric worldview.

• I recognize all four. I wouldn’t need to look any up first.  
• I’d weight their views but tilt most toward Larry Fink’s institutional-flow perspective, give Cathie Wood’s model a fair hearing, discount Dimon’s bank-bias, and largely set aside Schiff’s gold advocacy.
